risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Students were then randomized to experimental or control groups, using the random blocks method21 stratifying on gender (male/female) and self-reported attention deficit hyperactivity disorder status (yes, no), to either (a) immediate instruction in the TM program or (b) wait list/delayed start condition." Comment: The paper explicitly states use of the random blocks method for allocation, which indicates a random sequence generation approach. Although the exact algorithm is not spelled out in this report, block randomization is a recognized random method, and nothing suggests a non-random or predictable sequence was used.

Quote: "The treatment group allocations were concealed by the study statistician, with individual treatment group assignments revealed to the project manager only when study participants completed baseline testing." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment process that prevented the recruiters/enrolling staff from knowing the upcoming assignment before enrollment and baseline completion; therefore allocation concealment is adequately concealed and at low risk of selection bias.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a single-blind RCT comparing stress reduction with the TM program to wait-list controls" and "All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design." Comment: Participants and intervention personnel were not blinded; only outcome assessors were masked. Because the intervention was a behavioral stress-reduction program and outcomes included blood pressure, psychological distress, and coping, knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence both participant behavior and reporting, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design." Comment: The outcome assessors were explicitly masked to allocation. The primary outcome (blood pressure) was measured with an automated BP monitor, and the secondary outcomes were self-reported questionnaires administered by masked research staff. Because the assessors were blinded and the blood pressure measurement was objective/automated, knowledge of allocation was unlikely to bias outcome measurement.

Quote: "A limitation is that about 30% of the pretest sample was unavailable for post-testing. This concern is mitigated by the fact that across groups completers did not differ from non-completers on any baseline entry characteristics. Also there were no differences between groups on baseline primary and secondary outcomes in noncompleters." Comment: Although attrition was not trivial overall (207/298 completed), the paper states that dropout was balanced in terms of baseline characteristics and that non-completers did not differ between groups on outcome-related baseline variables. This makes attrition-related bias in the outcome comparison unlikely, so Low risk is appropriate under a permissive Cochrane judgement.
